body:
## Problem

Clippy can create and update meetings, but cannot forward meetings to new attendees or propose alternative meeting times (counter-proposals).

## What Graph Supports

**Forward meeting:**
- `HTTP POST /me/events/{id}/forward` — forward an existing meeting to additional recipients
- Body: `ToRecipients` array (email addresses)
- Exchange sends the meeting forward as a new invite from the original organizer

**Propose new time (Counter)** — Graph beta:
- `eventProposal` property on events (in beta)
- Allows attendees to propose alternative meeting times
- Organizer receives the counter-proposal and can accept/decline

**EWS SOAP:**
- `ForwardItem` operation — forward a calendar item to other recipients

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy meeting forward <eventId> --to newAttendee@company.com` — forward meeting to new person
- `clippy meeting propose-time <eventId> --new-time "2026-04-02 14:00"` — propose alternate time (beta)
- Note: meeting proposed times require the meeting organizer's side to accept — may not be actionable for PA, but the forward use case is

## Use Case

PA receives a meeting request, needs to add an additional colleague who should have been invited. PA forwards the meeting to that colleague on behalf of the executive.
title: Add meeting forward and proposed new time (counter) support

body:
## Updated: EWS SOAP may support this after all

The previous analysis stated this requires Exchange PowerShell. Upon further review:

**ServerReplyWithMessage** IS available in EWS SOAP `UpdateInboxRules` — it is simply not exposed by the Microsoft Graph REST API. This means Clippy could implement it via EWS SOAP if someone writes the `UpdateRuleOperation` with `ServerReplyWithMessage` action.

The limitation is **code complexity**, not permission level. The user's own token has the necessary rights — the EWS SOAP operation just needs to be written.

## Priority: Low / Experimental

This is technically achievable but low priority and complex to implement correctly. The EWS SOAP schema for this operation is non-trivial and requires careful XML construction.

## Implementation Sketch

```typescript
// src/lib/ews-client.ts
// In UpdateInboxRulesRequest:
export interface ServerReplyRuleAction {
  ServerReplyWithMessage: {
    Message: {
      Subject?: string;
      Body: { BodyType: 'Text' | 'HTML'; _: string };
    }
  }
}
// EWS SOAP envelope would include:
<UpdateInboxRulesRequest>
  <Operations>
    <SetRuleOperation>
      <Rule>
        <DisplayName>...</DisplayName>
        <Conditions>...</Conditions>
        <Actions>
          <ServerReplyWithMessage>
            <Message>
              <t:Subject>...</t:Subject>
              <t:Body BodyType="Text">...</t:Body>
            </Message>
          </ServerReplyWithMessage>
        </Actions>
      </Rule>
    </SetRuleOperation>
  </Operations>
</UpdateInboxRulesRequest>
```

## Current Workaround

Basic OOF templates via `PATCH /me/mailboxSettings` (issue #83).

## Labels

enhancement, Queue:Low, experimental, needs-ews-soap
title: Server-side auto-reply templates not available in Microsoft Graph (requires Exchange PowerShell)

body:
## Problem

Clippy has `findtime` for basic availability checking, but lacks the richer `getSchedule` and `findMeetingTimes` Graph APIs that provide AI-suggested optimal meeting times.

## What Graph Supports

**getSchedule** (Microsoft Graph):
- `POST /me/calendar/getSchedule` — free/busy for multiple users/distribution lists/resources for a time range
- Input: list of attendee emails + time range
- Output: merged free/busy calendar for each attendee, working hours, OOF status
- Much richer than the current EWS-based findtime

**findMeetingTimes** (AI-suggested times, Microsoft Graph beta):
- `POST /me/findMeetingTimes` — AI-suggested optimal meeting times
- Considers: attendee availability, preferred locations, meeting constraints (duration, time window)
- Returns: suggested meeting times with confidence scores and locations

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy freebusy user@company.com --from 2026-04-01 --to 2026-04-03` — richer than current findtime
- `clippy suggest-meeting "vip@company.com,team@company.com" --duration 60 --location "Conference Room"` — Graph `findMeetingTimes` equivalent
- Support for DL expansion in free/busy: `clippy freebusy "team@company.com"` -> expand DL -> check each member

## Note

Clippy already has `findtime` — this issue is about upgrading to use the Graph APIs for richer results, DL support, and AI suggestions.
title: Add free/busy queries and AI meeting time suggestions (getSchedule / findMeetingTimes)

body:
## Problem

Clippy's `find` command searches contacts, not the corporate directory. PAs frequently need to find any employee in the organization and email distribution lists.

## What Graph Supports

**People API** (not a true GAL, but close):
- `GET /me/people?$search="john"` — most relevant people to the user (collaboration graph)
- `GET /me/people/{id}` — person details

**Directory (GAL) Search:**
- `GET /users?$filter=startsWith(displayName,'john')` — search users in directory
  - Requires `User.Read.All` or `Directory.Read.All` (org-wide)
  - Returns: displayName, mail, jobTitle, department, officeLocation
- `GET /users/{id}/photo/$value` — user's photo

**Distribution List Expansion:**
- `GET /groups/{groupId}/members` — expand a DL to get all member email addresses
  - Requires `Group.Read.All` or `Group.Read` (depends on org policy)
- `GET /groups?$filter=startsWith(displayName,'team-')` — find DLs by name

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy people "john smith"` — search the directory (people API)
- `clippy gal "john"` — search global address list (requires higher permissions)
- `clippy dl expand group@company.com` — expand distribution list to member emails
- `clippy dl find "team-"` — find distribution lists by name
- `clippy user user@company.com` — show user details (title, department, phone)

## Current Gap

Clippy's `find` command searches EWS contacts (personal + picked GAL contacts). Not full org-wide directory.
title: Add distribution list expansion and GAL (directory) lookup

body:
## Problem

Clippy operates on demand — it can't react in real-time when new email arrives or calendar changes. PAs need push notifications for VIP emails, meeting changes, etc.

## What Graph Supports

**Subscriptions API** (Microsoft Graph webhooks):
- `POST /subscriptions` — create a subscription (webhook)
- Supported resources:
  - `mail` — new email (requires `Mail.Read`)
  - `event` — calendar changes (`Calendars.Read`)
  - `contact` — contact changes (`Contacts.Read`)
  - `todoTask` — To-Do changes (`Tasks.Read`)
  - `driveItem` — OneDrive file changes
- Max subscription lifetime: ~3 days (4230 minutes); requires renewal logic
- Webhook delivery to a registered endpoint URL

**EWS Streaming Notifications** (legacy, still functional):
- `Subscribe` with `StreamingNotifications` — long-running HTTP connection for push notifications
- Events: NewMail, ModifiedItems, DeletedItems, etc.
- More real-time than Graph webhooks (no polling/expiration)

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy subscribe --mail --webhook https://my-agent.com/hooks/email` — set up email notifications
- `clippy subscribe --calendar --webhook https://my-agent.com/hooks/calendar`
- `clippy subscribe list` — show active subscriptions
- `clippy subscribe renew <id>` — renew expiring subscription
- Internal: webhook receiver endpoint to handle notification payloads

## Use Cases

- "New email from CEO -> instantly notify PA via push"
- "Calendar changed on executive's calendar -> PA gets instant notification"
- "New task assigned in To-Do -> PA gets notified"

## Note

This is a significant architectural addition — requires a running webhook receiver, subscription renewal logic, and lifecycle management.
title: Add Graph subscription support for real-time push notifications

body:
## Problem

Clippy cannot set or read out-of-office / automatic reply messages for a mailbox. PAs need to manage their executive's OOF settings.

## What Graph Supports

**Automatic Replies** via mailbox settings (Microsoft Graph):
- `PATCH /me/mailboxSettings` with `automaticRepliesSetting`:
  - `status`: alwaysEnabled, scheduled, disabled
  - `internalReplyMessage` — message for internal senders
  - `externalReplyMessage` — message for external senders
  - `scheduledStartDateTime`, `scheduledEndDateTime` — for scheduled OOF
- `GET /me/mailboxSettings` — read current OOF status
- `delegateMeetingMessageDeliveryOptions` — control delegate delivery of meeting messages

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy oof status` — show current OOF settings
- `clippy oof set "I'm out of office until April 7" --external "Please contact my PA at..." --start 2026-03-28 --end 2026-04-07`
- `clippy oof disable` — turn off OOF

## Note

Requires delegate access to executive's mailbox, or PA manages their own OOF.
title: Add out-of-office (automatic reply) management

body:
## Problem

Clippy has no task management at all. PAs need to track action items from emails and meetings.

**IMPORTANT:** The Outlook Tasks API (`/me/outlook/tasks`) is **DEPRECATED** and stopped returning data on August 20, 2022. Any task integration must use the **Microsoft To-Do API**.

## What Graph Supports (Microsoft To-Do, v1.0)

- `GET /me/todo/lists` — list all To-Do task lists
- `GET /me/todo/lists/{listId}/tasks` — tasks in a list
- `POST /me/todo/lists/{listId}/tasks` — create task
- `PATCH /me/todo/lists/{listId}/tasks/{taskId}` — update (complete, change, flag)
- `DELETE /me/todo/lists/{listId}/tasks/{taskId}` — delete task
- `POST /me/todo/lists/{listId}/tasks/{taskId}/checklistItems` — add checklist subtask
- `linkedResource` — cross-reference to email/message that spawned the task (very PA-relevant!)
  - Useful pattern: "email from VIP -> create task linked to that email -> track completion"

**Task properties:**
- `title`, `body` (notes), `isReminderOn`, `reminderDateTime`
- `dueDateTime`, `startDateTime`
- `status`: notStarted, inProgress, completed, waiting, deferred
- `importance`: low, normal, high
- `linkedResources` — URLs linking back to source email/message
- `checklistItems` — subtask checklist items

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy todo lists` — list task lists
- `clippy todo --list "Work"` — show tasks in a list
- `clippy todo create "Follow up with VIP" --due 2026-04-01 --linked-resource msgId`
- `clippy todo complete <taskId>` — mark done
- `clippy todo add-checklist <taskId> "Call back"`
- Pattern: `--linked-email <msgId>` to link task back to originating email

## Why Not Planner?

Microsoft Planner (`/me/planner/tasks`) is for Office 365 Groups/Teams task management. To-Do is the personal task management API appropriate for PA workflows.
title: Add Microsoft To-Do task management (replace deprecated Outlook Tasks API)

body:
## Problem

Clippy cannot create, list, update, or delete server-side inbox rules. PAs need to set up automatic email sorting, forwarding, and flagging rules for their executives.

## What Graph Supports

**Message Rules API** (Microsoft Graph v1.0):
- `GET /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules` — list all rules
- `POST /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules` — create a rule
- `PATCH /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules/{id}` — update a rule
- `DELETE /me/mailFolders/inbox/messageRules/{id}` — delete a rule

**Supported Conditions (`messageRulePredicates`):**
- `bodyContains`, `subjectContains`, `senderContains`, `recipientContains`
- `fromAddresses`, `sentToAddresses`
- `importance`, `hasAttachments`, `headerContains`
- `isAutomaticForward`, `isMeetingRequest`, `isVoicemail`, `isApprovalRequest`
- `withinSizeRange`, `withinDateRange`
- `categories`, `messageActionFlag`, `sensitivity`

**Supported Actions (`messageRuleActions`):**
- `moveToFolder`, `copyToFolder`
- `delete`, `permanentDelete`
- `markAsRead`, `markImportance`
- `forwardToRecipients`, `forwardAsAttachmentToRecipients`
- `redirectToRecipients`
- `assignCategories`
- `stopProcessingRules`

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy rules` — list all inbox rules
- `clippy rules create --name "VIP" --from vip@company.com --move-to VIP`
- `clippy rules update <id> --add-action forward --forward-to pa@company.com`
- `clippy rules delete <id>`

## Limitation

Server-side **auto-reply templates** are NOT available in Graph. Auto-reply (vacation response) is handled separately via `PATCH /me/mailboxSettings` — see separate issue.
title: Add server-side inbox rules management (messageRules CRUD)

body:
## Problem

Clippy's `create-event --find-room` and `--list-rooms` only works against a known room name. It cannot discover available rooms, filter by building/capacity/equipment, or book resource mailboxes.

## What Graph Supports

**Places API** (Microsoft Graph v1.0):
- `GET /places` — list all place resources (rooms, room lists)
- `GET /place/roomLists` — list all room lists (e.g., "Building A Floor 3")
- `GET /place/roomLists/{id}/rooms` — rooms in a specific list
- Room properties: `displayName`, `emailAddress`, `address`, `geoCoordinates`, `capacity`, `bookingType` (standard vs reserved), `tags` (AV, Whiteboard, etc.), `building`, `floorNumber`

**Resource calendar booking:**
- Book a room by adding it as an attendee to an event (set `isOrganizer` + room email)
- `GET /users/{roomEmail}/calendar` — read room's free/busy

## What's Missing in Clippy

- `clippy rooms` — list all rooms (from Places API)
- `clippy rooms --building "Building A"` — filter rooms
- `clippy rooms --capacity 10+` — filter by capacity
- `clippy rooms --equipment Whiteboard,Video` — filter by tags
- `clippy room-calendar room@company.com --free-busy` — see a room's calendar/free-busy
- Better integration: when `--find-room` runs, automatically query Places API to get real room options

## Note

Clippy already has `--find-room` and `--list-rooms` in `create-event`. These should be enhanced to use the Places API instead of the current hardcoded/implicit approach.
title: Room discovery via Places API and resource calendar booking

body:
## Problem

Clippy has no way to manage Microsoft 365 delegate permissions or access another user's mailbox as a delegate. A PA fundamentally needs to act on behalf of their executive — reading their email, managing their calendar, sending from their address.

## What EWS/Graph Support

**EWS SOAP** (no Graph equivalent):
- `AddDelegate` — add a delegate with per-folder permission levels (Calendar, Inbox, Contacts, etc.)
- `GetDelegate` — retrieve current delegate settings
- `UpdateDelegate` — modify existing delegate permissions
- `RemoveDelegate` — remove a delegate

Permissions controllable per folder:
- `CalendarFolderPermissionLevel`: None, Owner, PublishingEditor, Editor, PublishingAuthor, Author, Reviewer, etc.
- `ViewPrivateItems`: boolean
- `DeliverMeetingRequests`: DelegatesAndMe, DelegatesOnly, DelegatesAndSendToMe, None

**Microsoft Graph** (modern REST):
- `GET /me/mailboxSettings/delegates` — list delegates
- `PATCH /me/mailboxSettings` with `delegateMeetingMessageDeliveryOptions` — control meeting message delivery
- Shared mailbox access: `GET /users/{execUPN}/mailFolders`, `GET /users/{execUPN}/calendar` — requires `Mail.ReadWrite` + `Calendars.ReadWrite` on the shared mailbox

**SendAs** (Exchange Admin-level, not API-accessible):
- Cannot be granted programmatically — requires `Add-RecipientPermission` via Exchange Admin PowerShell
- If PA already has SendAs granted by admin, `POST /me/sendMail` with `from` override works

## What's Missing in Clippy

- No delegate add/remove/update commands
- No shared mailbox context switching (acting as executive)
- No `clippy delegates list` / `clippy delegates add` / `clippy delegates remove`
- No `clippy --as user@company.com` or `clippy --mailbox user@company.com` for calendar/email (already has --mailbox for calendar write ops, but not for reading)

## Proposed Commands

- `clippy delegates list` — show current delegates and permissions
- `clippy delegates add user@company.com --calendar editor --inbox reviewer --view-private`
- `clippy delegates remove user@company.com`
- `clippy mail --as user@company.com` — read executive's inbox
- `clippy calendar --as user@company.com` — view executive's calendar
title: Add delegate management (AddDelegate/UpdateDelegate/RemoveDelegate) and shared mailbox support

body:
## Problem

`clippy calendar` (and all event commands) discards timezone information from Exchange, and provides no way to specify a timezone when creating or updating events.

### Data loss on read

In `parseCalendarItem()` (`src/lib/ews-client.ts`), the `TimeZone` field of `Start` and `End` is hardcoded to `'UTC'`:

```typescript
Start: { DateTime: start, TimeZone: 'UTC' },
End: { DateTime: end, TimeZone: 'UTC' },
```

The actual timezone sent by EWS (e.g., `"W. Europe Standard Time"`) is discarded. This means users cannot see that an event is in a specific timezone — it's always shown as UTC.

### No timezone on create/update

`createEvent()` and `updateEvent()` have no timezone option. The `--day` and time arguments are parsed into a JS `Date`, then converted via `toLocalISOString()`. There is no `--timezone` flag. This means:
- Events are created with whatever timezone `toLocalISOString()` produces (typically server local time or UTC)
- Users in non-UTC timezones cannot reliably create events at specific local times
- Recurring events in a specific timezone (e.g., "every Monday at 9am Pacific") cannot be expressed

### Hardcoded bias in availability API

`getScheduleViaOutlook()` and `isRoomFree()` hardcode `TimeZone` bias as `-60` (CET), which is wrong for users not in that timezone.

## EWS API support

EWS fully supports timezones:
- `Start` and `End` can include `TimeZoneContext` or per-item timezone info
- The `TimeZoneDefinition` type allows creating named timezone references
- `CreateItem` supports `<t:TimeZone>` child element to set the event's timezone

## Fix

1. Store the actual `TimeZone` from EWS in `CalendarEvent.Start.TimeZone` and `CalendarEvent.End.TimeZone` (parse it from the response, don't hardcode)
2. Add a `--timezone` option to `create-event` and `update-event` (e.g., `--timezone "Pacific Standard Time"`)
3. Pass the timezone to EWS in `CreateItem` / `UpdateItem` via the appropriate element
4. Fix `getScheduleViaOutlook()` to use the user's actual timezone rather than a hardcoded CET bias

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: Timezone information lost and no timezone option for event creation

body:
## Problem

When a recurring event series has exceptions — instances that were modified, deleted, or have notes attached — clippy provides no visibility into these. The user cannot see:
- Which occurrences were changed from the original series pattern
- Which occurrences were deleted from the series
- What the changes were (new time, new location, etc.)

## Root cause

`getCalendarEvents()` does not request the following EWS calendar properties:
- `calendar:ModifiedOccurrences` — list of changed occurrence items
- `calendar:DeletedOccurrences` — list of deleted occurrence items
- `calendar:FirstOccurrence` — first occurrence details (start/end)
- `calendar:LastOccurrence` — last occurrence details (start/end)

The `CalendarEvent` type also has no fields for these.

## EWS API behavior

When you use `FindItem` with `CalendarView`, EWS returns all occurrences within the date range, including exceptions (modified occurrences appear as separate `CalendarItem` elements with an `OrignalItemId` linking them to the series). Deleted occurrences are NOT returned in `CalendarView` — you need to request `DeletedOccurrences` explicitly.

The `ModifiedOccurrences` and `DeletedOccurrences` properties on the recurring master item contain `OccurrenceInfo` elements with the details.

## Expected behavior

`clippy calendar -v --id <recurring-event-id>` (or a hypothetical `clippy event --show-series <id>`) should display:
- "Series: Weekly, every Monday until 2026-06-30"
- "First occurrence: 2026-03-02"
- "Last occurrence: 2026-06-29"
- "Modified: 2026-03-23 (time changed to 14:00)"
- "Deleted: 2026-04-06 (Easter Monday)"

## CLI opportunities

The exception/edge cases also affect `--update` and `--delete`:
- Before updating/deleting an occurrence, warn if that occurrence has been modified from the series master
- When deleting an occurrence that has an exception, ask for confirmation
- Consider a `clippy event exceptions <id>` command to list all exceptions in a series

## Dependencies

Issue #73 (viewing recurrence info) must be resolved first, as it adds the field infrastructure needed here.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: No visibility into recurring event exceptions (modified/deleted occurrences)

body:
## Problem

`clippy delete-event` cannot delete a single occurrence of a recurring event. There is no `--occurrence`, `--instance`, or `--scope` flag. Every delete operation targets the entire series.

When `deleteEvent()` is called, it sends `DeleteType="MoveToDeletedItems"` with `SendMeetingCancellations="SendToNone"`. This will delete the event (or entire series if it's a recurring master) but has no way to target a specific occurrence.

## Missing options

- `--occurrence <index>` — Delete only the Nth occurrence
- `--instance <ISO date>` — Delete only the occurrence on a specific date
- `--scope all` — Delete entire series (current default)
- `--scope this` — Delete only this occurrence
- `--scope future` — Delete this and all future occurrences

## EWS API support

To delete a single occurrence, you need the `OccurrenceItemId` for that occurrence (obtained via `CalendarView` on the series). Then use EWS `DeleteItem` with `DeleteType="MoveToDeletedItems"` and `SendMeetingCancellations="SendToOnlyThisOccurrence"` (or similar scoped cancellation).

EWS also supports `CancelCalendarItem` (used by `cancelEvent()`) with scoped cancellation options:
- `SendToAllAndSaveCopy` — cancels entire series
- `SendToOnlyThisAndSaveCopy` — cancels only the specific occurrence
- `SendToAllAndForwardAndSaveCopy` — for delegate scenarios

## User experience gap

When a user runs `clippy delete-event --id <id>` on what turns out to be a recurring event, clippy silently deletes or cancels the entire series without warning. There is no confirmation prompt like "This is a recurring event. Delete [this occurrence] / [all occurrences] / cancel?"

This could result in accidentally cancelling a whole series of meetings instead of just one instance.

## Related

Issue #73 (viewing recurrence info) is needed to surface that an event is recurring before attempting deletion. Issue #75 covers the same gap for the update command.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: Cannot delete a single occurrence of a recurring event

body:
## Problem

`clippy update-event` cannot modify a single occurrence of a recurring event. There is no `--occurrence`, `--scope`, or similar flag. All updates go to the entire series.

When you call `updateEvent()` in the EWS client, it sends `SendMeetingInvitationsOrCancellations="SendToAllAndSaveCopy"` (or `SendToNone` if no attendees). EWS interprets this as updating the recurring series master, which propagates changes to all future (and optionally past) occurrences.

## Missing options

- `--occurrence <index>` — Update only the Nth occurrence (e.g., `--occurrence 5`)
- `--instance <ISO date>` — Update only the occurrence on a specific date
- `--scope all` — Update all occurrences (current default)
- `--future` — Update this and all future occurrences

## EWS API support

EWS UpdateItem supports an `UpdateItem` element with `ConflictResolution` and `SendMeetingInvitationsOrCancellations` attributes. To update a single occurrence:

1. You need the `OccurrenceItemId` (not the `ItemId` of the recurring master) — EWS returns these when you fetch a recurring event with `CalendarView`
2. Pass the `OccurrenceItemId` instead of `ItemId` in the `UpdateItem` call
3. EWS will then only update that specific occurrence

Alternatively, use `UpdateItem` with a `UpdateOperation` that sets the `SendMeetingInvitationsOrCancellations` appropriately and uses the occurrence's item ID.

## Expected behavior

```
# Update only the 5th occurrence
clippy update-event --id <recurring-event-id> --occurrence 5 --title "New Title"

# Update only the occurrence on a specific date
clippy update-event --id <recurring-event-id> --instance 2026-04-15 --title "New Title"
```

## Related

This is issue #73 (viewing recurrence info) is a prerequisite — you need to see which occurrences exist before you can target them individually.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: Cannot modify or delete a single occurrence of a recurring event

body:
## Problem

The `RecurrencePattern` type in `src/lib/ews-client.ts` defines six pattern types:

```
Type: 'Daily' | 'Weekly' | 'AbsoluteMonthly' | 'RelativeMonthly' | 'AbsoluteYearly' | 'RelativeYearly';
```

However, `buildRecurrenceXml()` only has explicit cases for `Daily`, `Weekly`, `AbsoluteMonthly`, and `AbsoluteYearly`. `RelativeMonthly` and `RelativeYearly` fall through to the `default` case, which emits `<t:DailyRecurrence>` — silently generating the wrong recurrence type.

## Impact

Users who try to use `--repeat monthly` with a relative day (e.g., "the second Tuesday") have no way to do so. More critically, any user who somehow specifies `RelativeMonthly` or `RelativeYearly` will get a silently incorrect recurrence created.

## EWS API support

EWS supports:

- `RelativeMonthlyRecurrence` — e.g., "every 2 months on the third Monday"
  ```
  <t:RelativeMonthlyRecurrence>
    <t:Interval>2</t:Interval>
    <t:DaysOfWeek>Monday</t:DaysOfWeek>
    <t:DayOfWeekIndex>Third</t:DayOfWeekIndex>
  </t:RelativeMonthlyRecurrence>
  ```
- `RelativeYearlyRecurrence` — e.g., "every year on the third Monday of September"
  ```
  <t:RelativeYearlyRecurrence>
    <t:DaysOfWeek>Monday</t:DaysOfWeek>
    <t:DayOfWeekIndex>Third</t:DayOfWeekIndex>
    <t:Month>September</t:Month>
  </t:RelativeYearlyRecurrence>
  ```

The `RecurrencePattern` type already has an `Index` field (`'First' | 'Second' | 'Third' | 'Fourth' | 'Last'`) intended for this purpose, but it is never used in `buildRecurrenceXml()`.

## Fix

Extend `buildRecurrenceXml()` in `src/lib/ews-client.ts` to handle `RelativeMonthly` and `RelativeYearly` cases. The `Index` field should map to `t:DayOfWeekIndex` and `DaysOfWeek` should be used for the day name.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: RelativeMonthly and RelativeYearly recurrence patterns silently broken

body:
## Problem

When listing calendar events with `clippy calendar` or `clippy update-event`, recurring events are shown as ordinary single events. There is no indication that an event is part of a series, what the recurrence pattern is, or how many remaining occurrences exist.

## Root Cause

`getCalendarEvents()` in `src/lib/ews-client.ts` uses the `Default` base shape and only explicitly requests a small set of additional properties. It does not fetch:
- `calendar:Recurrence` — the recurrence pattern/range
- `calendar:IsRecurring` — whether the item is recurring
- `calendar:FirstOccurrence` / `calendar:LastOccurrence` — series bounds
- `calendar:ModifiedOccurrences` — exception instances that were changed
- `calendar:DeletedOccurrences` — exception instances that were deleted

The `CalendarEvent` type also has no fields for recurrence data.

## Expected behavior

`clippy calendar -v` should show something like "Weekly, every Monday until 2026-06-30" when viewing a recurring event. And `clippy update-event --id <id>` should show recurrence info when displaying an existing recurring event.

## EWS API support

EWS `FindItem` with `CalendarView` returns `CalendarItem` elements for the occurrences in the date range. To see recurrence info, add `AdditionalProperties` for `calendar:Recurrence` in the `ItemShape`. Note that with `CalendarView`, EWS returns individual occurrence items — to get the series master itself, a separate `GetItem` call or `FindItem` with `Traversal="Deep"` may be needed.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: Cannot view recurrence info for existing recurring events

labels.name: Queue:Medium
labels.description: Medium priority — bugs, reliability issues, partial functionality loss

title: [Reliability] resolveAuth caches tokens without validating JWT structure — corrupted cache causes opaque auth failures

body:
## Finding

**File:** `src/lib/auth.ts`, `src/lib/graph-auth.ts`

**Severity:** Low

## Description

`resolveAuth()` and `resolveGraphAuth()` silently fall back to `EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN` when `GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN` is not set:

```typescript
// graph-auth.ts
const envRefreshToken = process.env.GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN || process.env.EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN;
```

This is documented in the error message:
> 'Missing EWS_CLIENT_ID and/or GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN (or EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN) in environment.'

However, using the same refresh token for both EWS and Graph is risky because:

1. **Scope mismatch**: EWS tokens have `https://outlook.office365.com/EWS.AccessAsUser.All` scope, while Graph tokens have `https://graph.microsoft.com/...` scope. A token minted for one API may not work for the other.

2. **Security confusion**: The error message says "GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN (or EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN)" which implies they're interchangeable, but they're not.

3. **Silent fallback**: If `GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN` is expired but `EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN` is valid, the code will try to use the EWS token with Graph scopes and fail — with an opaque "Graph token refresh failed" error.

## Recommended Fix

1. **Do not fall back** to `EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN` for Graph auth — require `GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN` explicitly.
2. Update the error message to clearly state both tokens are required:
   ```
   Missing GRAPH_REFRESH_TOKEN in environment. 
   Note: EWS_REFRESH_TOKEN cannot be used for Graph operations.
   ```
3. Add a warning if the fallback token is used (for migration assistance during the transition).

labels.name: Queue:Medium
labels.description: Medium priority — bugs, reliability issues, partial functionality loss

body:
## Finding

**File:** `src/lib/graph-client.ts`

**Severity:** Low

## Description

`encodeGraphSearchQuery()` is documented as encoding a query for Graph's AQS (Advanced Query Syntax) search, but it only encodes characters that would break URL construction — it does not encode apostrophes:

```typescript
function encodeGraphSearchQuery(query: string): string {
  return encodeURIComponent(query).replace(/[!'()*]/g, (char) => `%${char.charCodeAt(0).toString(16).toUpperCase()}`);
}
```

The `replace()` pattern includes `!`, `'`, `(`, `)`, `*` — but `'` (apostrophe) is in the set of characters being replaced. However, the existing test confirms this works:

```typescript
await searchFiles(token, "') and name='anything");
// Expects: /me/drive/root/search(q='%27%29%20and%20name%3D%27anything')
```

The test shows single quotes ARE encoded to `%27`. So this actually works correctly. However, the implementation is confusing and fragile — `encodeURIComponent` already encodes most characters, and the subsequent `.replace(/[!'()*]/g, ...)` seems redundant for single quotes (since `encodeURIComponent` already encodes them).

The real question is whether Graph's AQS search handles double quotes differently from single quotes. Graph's `search(q='...')` function uses AQS, and the test covers the edge case correctly.

## Recommendation

Keep the current encoding behavior (it has test coverage), but add a comment explaining why apostrophes are re-encoded:

```typescript
/**
 * Encode a query string for Graph Drive search.
 * encodeURIComponent encodes most characters, but AQS search uses single-quoted
 * strings in the URL path, so apostrophes and parentheses must also be encoded
 * to prevent query syntax injection.
 */
function encodeGraphSearchQuery(query: string): string {
  return encodeURIComponent(query).replace(/[!'()*]/g, (char) => `%${char.charCodeAt(0).toString(16).toUpperCase()}`);
}
```

No code change needed — just a documentation comment for maintainability.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: [Tech Debt] encodeGraphSearchQuery lacks JSDoc — purpose of re-encoding after encodeURIComponent is unclear

body:
## Finding

**Files:** `src/commands/respond.ts`, `src/commands/delete-event.ts`, `src/commands/update-event.ts`

**Severity:** Medium

## Description

Several commands call `getCalendarEvents()` with a hardcoded date range that may be **too short**, silently returning empty results:

### respond.ts

```typescript
const futureDate = new Date(now);
futureDate.setDate(futureDate.getDate() + 30); // Exactly 30 days
const result = await getCalendarEvents(authResult.token!, now.toISOString(), futureDate.toISOString());
```

### delete-event.ts and update-event.ts

```typescript
const baseDate = parseDay(options.day);
const startOfDay = new Date(baseDate);
startOfDay.setHours(0, 0, 0, 0);
const endOfDay = new Date(baseDate);
endOfDay.setHours(23, 59, 59, 999);
const result = await getCalendarEvents(authResult.token!, startOfDay.toISOString(), endOfDay.toISOString());
```

### Issues

1. **`respond` command uses 30 days exactly**: If today is March 28 and there's an invitation on April 27 (exactly 30 days), it falls outside the range (March 28 to April 27 00:00). Using `now + 31 days` or `now + 1 month` would be more robust.

2. **Single-day range for delete/update**: Using `--day nextweek` queries only the single day of `nextweek` (which is Monday). If the event spans Monday midnight boundary, it may be missed. Consider querying a wider window or accepting the limitation with a warning.

3. **All-day events**: An all-day event on `nextweek` Monday that starts at midnight UTC may appear to start on the previous day when interpreted in a different timezone.

## Recommended Fix

1. In `respond.ts`: change `futureDate.setDate(futureDate.getDate() + 30)` to `futureDate.setDate(futureDate.getDate() + 31)` or use a calendar-month approach.

2. Add timezone-awareness: use `Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone` to determine the user's timezone and adjust the query window accordingly.

3. Document the `--day` limitation for multi-day events in `delete-event` and `update-event`.

labels.name: Queue:Medium
labels.description: Medium priority — bugs, reliability issues, partial functionality loss

title: [Reliability] respond command may miss invitations near 30-day boundary and events near day boundaries

body:
## Finding

**File:** `src/lib/ews-client.ts`

**Severity:** Medium

## Description

The `getScheduleViaOutlook()` function has two reliability issues:

### 1. Hardcoded timezone bias

```typescript
<t:Bias>-60</t:Bias>
// ...
<t:DaylightTime>
// ...
  <t:Bias>-60</t:Bias>
```

The timezone bias is hardcoded to `-60` (CET/CEST — Central European Time). This will produce incorrect results for users in other timezones, and it does not account for DST transitions within the date range being queried.

### 2. Empty suggestions → misleading "No available times" for the entire range

When no suggestions are returned (which is the normal case when no free slots exist), the code sets ALL schedules to "Busy" with a fake "No available times" slot:

```typescript
if (freeSlots.length === 0) {
  for (const schedule of schedules) {
    schedule.scheduleItems = [
      {
        status: 'Busy',
        start: { dateTime: startDateTime, timeZone: 'W. Europe Standard Time' },
        end: { dateTime: endDateTime, timeZone: 'W. Europe Standard Time' },
        subject: 'No available times'
      }
    ];
  }
}
```

This conflates "no suggestions found" with "entire range is busy". In reality, `getScheduleViaOutlook()` primarily fetches **MergedFreeBusy** data (free/busy view), not room suggestions. When the SuggestionsView returns nothing, it does not mean the whole range is busy — it means the suggestion algorithm found no good meeting times, but the FreeBusyView data is available in the same response and should be used.

### 3. getFreeBusy uses getCalendarEvents instead of GetUserAvailabilityRequest

`getFreeBusy()` fetches calendar items via `getCalendarEvents` and derives free/busy from the calendar view. This only returns **organizer's own events**, not the actual free/busy information for arbitrary email addresses. For checking if a room or person is free, `getScheduleViaOutlook()` should be used.

## Recommended Fixes

1. Make the timezone bias configurable or derive it from `Intl.DateTimeFormat().resolvedOptions().timeZone`.
2. When `freeSlots.length === 0`, fall back to the `FreeBusyView` data in the response XML instead of creating fake "Busy" entries.
3. Add a separate `getFreeBusyForEmails()` function that calls `GetUserAvailabilityRequest` with `FreeBusyView` (not `DetailedMerged`) for the requested attendees.

labels.name: Queue:Medium
labels.description: Medium priority — bugs, reliability issues, partial functionality loss

title: [Reliability] getScheduleViaOutlook hardcoded timezone and misleading empty-suggestions handling

title: No test suite — all 13 commands are untested

body:
**Severity:** Medium

**Problem:**
The CI pipeline runs `typecheck`, `biome lint`, and `biome format` but does **not** run any tests. There is no test suite present in the repository at all.

Additionally, CI is missing:
- **No `package-lock.json` committed** (only `bun.lock` is tracked). Lockfile drift between CI and local dev environments is silent and unpredictable.
- **`bunx biome format .`** is listed as a CI step, but **format check (`bunx biome format --check .`) is absent** — meaning CI will silently reformat code on every push rather than failing when format violations exist.

**Impact:**
- No regression detection for broken functionality.
- Lockfile inconsistencies can cause CI to pass while local environments behave differently.
- The format step actively modifies files in CI on every push, which is not idempotent and can cause confusing git histories.

**Suggested fix:**
1. Add a test suite (e.g., Vitest) with at least smoke tests for auth resolution, EWS client responses, and command argument parsing.
2. Add `bun.lock` to `.gitignore` (Bun projects should only track `bun.lock` and ignore `package-lock.json`) — or ensure both are consistently handled.
3. Replace `bunx biome format .` in CI with `bun run format:check` (which runs `biome format --max-diagnostics=none .`) to fail CI on format violations rather than silently reformatting.

labels.name: Queue:Low
labels.description: Low priority — enhancements, tech debt, nice-to-haves

title: [Medium] CI: no test suite and format step silently reformats instead of failing
